site.title: syllogism.info
site.tagline: A map of arguments in analytic philosophy
site.description: Every premise is itself a claim. Click any premise to see the arguments for it, all the way down to the root premises.

psr.text: Every contingent fact has an explanation (the Principle of Sufficient Reason)

bccf.text: There is a Big Conjunctive Contingent Fact (BCCF): the conjunction of all contingent facts

bccf-needs-necessary.text: Any explanation of the BCCF must involve the causal activity of a necessary being

necessary-being.text: A necessary being whose activity explains all contingent facts exists

necessary-being-is-god.text: If a necessary being whose activity explains all contingent facts exists, then God exists

causal-principle.text: Whatever begins to exist has a cause of its beginning

universe-caused.text: The universe has a cause of its beginning

kalam-bridge.text: If the universe has a cause of its beginning, then a personal, transcendent creator exists

no-actual-infinite.text: An actually infinite number of things cannot exist

beginningless-past-infinite.text: A beginningless series of past events would be an actually infinite number of things

bgv-theorem.text: A universe that has, on average, been expanding throughout its history cannot be past-eternal (the Borde–Guth–Vilenkin theorem)

universe-expanding.text: Our universe has, on average, been expanding throughout its history

ft-data.text: The fundamental constants and quantities of our universe are fine-tuned for the existence of life

ft-trilemma.text: The fine-tuning of the universe is due to physical necessity, chance, or design

ft-not-necessity.text: The fine-tuning of the universe is not due to physical necessity

ft-not-chance.text: The fine-tuning of the universe is not due to chance

ft-design.text: The fine-tuning of the universe is due to design

ft-designer-god.text: If the fine-tuning of the universe is due to design, then God exists

gratuitous-evil.text: There exist instances of intense suffering which an omnipotent, omniscient being could have prevented without thereby losing some greater good or permitting some evil equally bad or worse

rowe-theological-premise.text: An omniscient, wholly good being would prevent the occurrence of any intense suffering it could, unless it could not do so without thereby losing some greater good or permitting some evil equally bad or worse

inscrutable-evils.text: There are evils — such as a fawn burning slowly to death in a remote forest fire — for which prolonged reflection reveals no outweighing good

noseeum-inference.text: If prolonged reflection reveals no outweighing good served by an evil, that is good (defeasible) evidence that there is none

god-def.text: If God exists, God is omnipotent and wholly good

good-opposes-evil.text: A wholly good being always eliminates evil as far as it can

omnipotence-no-limits.text: There are no limits to what an omnipotent being can do

god-implies-no-evil.text: If God exists, evil does not exist

loving-god-open.text: If a perfectly loving God exists, God is open to a personal relationship with every finite person capable of one

relationship-requires-belief.text: A person cannot be in a personal relationship with God without believing that God exists

loving-god-no-nonresistant-nonbelief.text: If a perfectly loving God exists, no capable person is a nonresistant nonbeliever

nonresistant-nonbelief.text: There are capable persons who are nonresistant nonbelievers

god-perfectly-loving.text: If God exists, God is perfectly loving

honest-doubters.text: There are former believers who lost their belief through honest inquiry and would welcome its return

isolated-nontheists.text: There are people who have never so much as encountered the idea of God

doubters-not-resisting.text: Honest doubters and isolated nontheists are not resisting a personal relationship with God

hands-are-external.text: Hands are objects external to our minds

two-external-objects.text: At least two external objects exist

sensory-coherence.text: Our sensory experiences are richly detailed, coherent, and lawfully ordered

rwh-best-explanation.text: The real-world hypothesis explains the order and coherence of our experience better than any skeptical rival (dreams, demons, simulations)

ibe-principle.text: We are justified in believing the hypothesis that best explains our evidence

contingency.title: The Argument from Contingency

source.title: The Leibnizian Cosmological Argument
source.container: The Blackwell Companion to Natural Theology, ed. W. L. Craig & J. P. Moreland

contingency.notes: The Leibnizian version of the cosmological argument. The main battlegrounds are the Principle of Sufficient Reason itself and the 'gap problem' of step 6: why the necessary being should be God rather than, say, a necessarily existing physical state.

kalam.title: The Kalam Cosmological Argument

source.authors: William Lane Craig & James D. Sinclair
source.title: The Kalam Cosmological Argument
source.container: The Blackwell Companion to Natural Theology, ed. W. L. Craig & J. P. Moreland

kalam.notes: Revived from medieval Islamic philosophy by Craig. Step 4 is defended by conceptual analysis: a cause of all space, time, and matter must be spaceless, timeless, immaterial, and — Craig argues — personal, since only free agency can explain a temporal effect of a timeless cause.

fine-tuning.title: The Fine-Tuning Argument

source.title: The Teleological Argument: An Exploration of the Fine-Tuning of the Universe
source.container: The Blackwell Companion to Natural Theology, ed. W. L. Craig & J. P. Moreland

fine-tuning.notes: Presented here in the popular trilemma form; Collins's own formulation is a likelihood argument (fine-tuning is far more probable given theism than given a naturalistic single universe). The chance disjunct is where the multiverse objection enters.

evidential-evil.title: The Evidential Problem of Evil

source.title: The Problem of Evil and Some Varieties of Atheism

evidential-evil.notes: Rowe's classic formulation. Unlike the logical problem, it claims only that gratuitous evil probably exists, so the conclusion is probabilistic. The skeptical theist response attacks premise 1 via our cognitive limitations.

logical-evil.title: The Logical Problem of Evil
logical-evil.notes: Mackie claimed the theistic set of beliefs is internally inconsistent. Plantinga's free will defence (1974) is widely held to have shown premises 2 and 3 are not necessary truths, moving the debate to the evidential version.

divine-hiddenness.title: The Argument from Divine Hiddenness
divine-hiddenness.notes: The other major contemporary argument for atheism alongside the problem of evil. Everything turns on whether nonresistant nonbelief actually occurs and on whether a loving God might have reasons to permit temporary hiddenness.

moorean-proof.title: Moore’s Proof of an External World
moorean-proof.notes: Moore held the premises are more certain than any skeptical principle that could be used against them. The standard complaint is that the proof is question-begging or fails to transmit warrant (Wright 2002); Moore's reply is that a proof need not convince the skeptic to be a proof.

ibe-external-world.title: The Explanationist Argument for the External World

source.title: Cartesian Skepticism and Inference to the Best Explanation

ibe-external-world.notes: Descends from Russell's 'instinctive belief' response in The Problems of Philosophy (1912). Vogel argues the real-world hypothesis beats skeptical rivals on explanatory virtues like simplicity, since skeptical hypotheses must smuggle in an isomorphic copy of the world to explain the same data.

actual-infinite.title: The Argument from the Impossibility of an Actual Infinite

source.authors: William Lane Craig & James D. Sinclair
source.title: The Kalam Cosmological Argument
source.container: The Blackwell Companion to Natural Theology, ed. W. L. Craig & J. P. Moreland

actual-infinite.notes: Defended with thought experiments like Hilbert's Hotel, which are said to show that an actual (as opposed to potential) infinite leads to absurdities. Critics respond that Cantorian set theory renders the actual infinite coherent.

bgv-expansion.title: The Argument from Cosmic Expansion

source.authors: Arvind Borde, Alan H. Guth & Alexander Vilenkin
source.title: Inflationary Spacetimes Are Incomplete in Past Directions

bgv-expansion.notes: The BGV theorem shows that any universe with a positive average expansion rate is geodesically incomplete to the past. Whether past-incompleteness amounts to an absolute beginning — and whether exotic models escape the theorem's assumptions — is disputed.

rowe-fawn.title: The Inference from Inscrutable Evil

source.title: The Problem of Evil and Some Varieties of Atheism

rowe-fawn.notes: Rowe's support for the factual premise of the evidential argument. Wykstra's 'noseeum' objection targets step 2: given the gap between our cognition and God's, our failure to see an outweighing good is poor evidence that there isn't one.

nonresistant-cases.title: The Case for Nonresistant Nonbelief

source.title: The Wisdom to Doubt: A Justification of Religious Skepticism

nonresistant-cases.notes: Schellenberg's fuller taxonomy of nonbelief includes reflective former believers, lifelong seekers, converts to nontheistic religions, and isolated nontheists — categories meant to make blaming all nonbelief on resistance implausible.
